name: Ehecatl
description: Ehecatl, whose name translates to "Wind" in Nahuatl, is a prominent deity within the Mesoamerican pantheon, particularly revered by the cultures of central Mexico, including the Aztec. He is most commonly recognized as the wind god, embodying the vital force that animates the cosmos. Ehecatl is often depicted as a feathered serpent, a visual representation connecting him to Quetzalcoatl, with whom he is frequently associated, sometimes even considered an aspect or manifestation. His influence extends beyond mere atmospheric phenomena; he is viewed as a harbinger of rain, vital for agriculture, and a cleanser who sweeps away impurities. He is also considered a god of creation, responsible for blowing life into the newly formed sun and humanity. Temples dedicated to Ehecatl were often round, designed to minimize wind resistance, and his worship involved offerings and rituals intended to appease and harness his powerful force. His presence underscores the importance of wind as a life-giving and transformative element in the Mesoamerican worldview.
subtitle: The Wind

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Fifth Sun
keyMyths.description: In the Aztec creation myth, after the previous suns had been destroyed, the gods gathered to create the Fifth Sun. Two gods, Nanahuatzin and Tecciztecatl, volunteered to sacrifice themselves. However, only Nanahuatzin bravely leaped into the fire, becoming the sun. Tecciztecatl hesitated but eventually followed, becoming the moon. The sun was motionless, and Ehecatl was called upon to blow it into motion, giving it life and allowing it to traverse the sky, thus beginning the current era. This myth highlights Ehecatl's crucial role in the ongoing creation and maintenance of the world.
keyMyths.source: Sahagún, Bernardino de. *General History of the Things of New Spain*.

keyMyths.title: Ehecatl and Mayahuel
keyMyths.description: Ehecatl, desiring to be with the goddess Mayahuel, who was kept imprisoned by her grandmother, flew down to earth as wind and uprooted the tree where she was held. He carried her away to the heavens, where they became intertwined as a double-branched tree. When her grandmother discovered their escape, she tore the tree apart, scattering the branches. Mayahuel's branch became the first maguey plant, from which pulque is made, linking Ehecatl to fertility and the gift of sustenance.
keyMyths.source: Bierhorst, John. *Mythology of Mexico and Central America*.

keyMyths.title: Ehecatl as a Cleansing Force
keyMyths.description: Ehecatl is believed to be a powerful force for purification, sweeping away evil and disease. Before the arrival of Tlaloc, the rain god, Ehecatl would clear the skies, removing any lingering malevolent influences that might contaminate the rain. This act ensured that the rain would be beneficial and nourishing to the earth, promoting growth and prosperity. This emphasizes Ehecatl's role in maintaining cosmic balance and protecting humanity from harm.
keyMyths.source: Miller, Mary, and Karl Taube. *An Illustrated Dictionary of the Gods and Symbols of Ancient Mexico and the Maya*.

associations.name: Wind
associations.significance: Ehecatl's primary association, representing breath, life, and movement.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: Connected to Quetzalcoatl, symbolizing wisdom, knowledge, and the connection between earth and sky.

associations.name: Creation
associations.significance: As the wind, he is essential for creation, sweeping away the old and bringing in the new.

associations.name: Communication
associations.significance: The wind carries messages and prayers between humans and the gods.

associations.name: Venus
associations.significance: Linked to Quetzalcoatl as the morning star, Ehecatl heralds the dawn.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: Represents the singular force of the wind, a fundamental element.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Ehecatl was often offered quail, butterflies, and other small creatures carried by the wind. Incense was also burned to honor him, the smoke rising like the wind.

worshipPractices.name: Temples
worshipPractices.description: Round temples were dedicated to Ehecatl, their shape allowing the wind to flow freely around them. These structures symbolized the cyclical nature of the wind and its omnipresence.

festivals.name: Tozoztontli
festivals.description: A festival dedicated to agriculture and the renewal of life, where Ehecatl's role in bringing the winds necessary for growth was celebrated.

festivals.name: Ehecailhuitl
festivals.description: Specifically dedicated to Ehecatl, involving dances, offerings, and rituals to ensure favorable winds for the coming season. The exact date varied depending on the Aztec calendar.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ehecatl's image and symbolism persist in Mexican art, literature, and cultural identity, representing the power of nature and the enduring legacy of pre-Columbian beliefs. His feathered serpent form is a popular motif.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Ehecatl's origins are intertwined with the creation myths of Mesoamerica. He is often described as an aspect or manifestation of Quetzalcoatl, the feathered serpent god. In some accounts, Ehecatl is born from the breath of the supreme creator god, Ometeotl, highlighting his role as a fundamental force of the universe. His birth is not a singular event but rather a continuous process, as the wind is ever-present and constantly renewed. The concept of Ehecatl predates the Aztec civilization, with evidence of wind god worship found in earlier cultures such as the Olmec and Teotihuacan. These early representations laid the groundwork for the more elaborate depictions and myths associated with Ehecatl in later Mesoamerican societies. His connection to Quetzalcoatl solidifies his importance as a key figure in the pantheon, representing not just the wind but also knowledge, wisdom, and the cycle of life and death.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Ehecatl is featured in several important Mesoamerican myths. One prominent story tells of his role in the creation of humanity. In this myth, the gods decide to create humans, but they need sustenance. Ehecatl, as the wind, blows away the mountains that are hiding the maize, allowing humans to access this vital food source. Another significant myth involves Ehecatl's love affair with Mayahuel, the goddess of maguey (agave). To escape the wrath of Mayahuel's grandmother, they transform themselves into a tree. However, the grandmother discovers them and breaks the tree, leaving Mayahuel dead. Ehecatl, heartbroken, gathers her remains and buries them, from which the maguey plant springs forth. This story highlights Ehecatl's connection to fertility and the origins of pulque, a sacred alcoholic beverage. These myths demonstrate Ehecatl's active role in shaping the world and his interactions with other deities, emphasizing his importance in the Mesoamerican worldview.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Ehecatl was widely worshipped throughout Mesoamerica, with dedicated temples and rituals performed in his honor. His temples were often round, a unique design that allowed the wind to circulate freely. These circular structures symbolized the cyclical nature of the wind and its constant movement. Priests of Ehecatl wore conical hats and masks resembling bird beaks, representing the wind's ability to soar through the air. Sacrifices were offered to Ehecatl, typically consisting of quail, butterflies, and other small creatures associated with the wind. Incense was burned as an offering, the smoke rising towards the heavens, carrying prayers and messages to the gods. During festivals dedicated to Ehecatl, dances were performed to mimic the movement of the wind, and elaborate costumes were worn to represent the deity. The cult of Ehecatl was particularly strong in central Mexico, where the Aztecs revered him as a powerful force of nature and a vital aspect of their cosmology. His worship aimed to ensure favorable winds for agriculture and to maintain balance in the cosmos.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Ehecatl's legacy extends beyond the pre-Columbian era, influencing modern Mexican culture and identity. His image as a feathered serpent, often depicted with a prominent beak-like mask, remains a recognizable symbol of Mesoamerican heritage. Ehecatl's association with Quetzalcoatl further enhances his cultural significance, as Quetzalcoatl is one of the most important deities in the Mesoamerican pantheon. Representations of Ehecatl can be found in contemporary art, literature, and popular culture, serving as a reminder of the rich history and complex belief systems of ancient Mexico. Furthermore, the concept of Ehecatl as the wind continues to resonate with people, representing the power of nature and the importance of balance in the environment. His influence can also be seen in modern interpretations of Mesoamerican spirituality, where he is often invoked as a symbol of change, renewal, and the interconnectedness of all things. Ehecatl's enduring presence in Mexican culture demonstrates the lasting impact of pre-Columbian beliefs and their continued relevance in the modern world.

shortDescription: Ehecatl is the Aztec wind god, often depicted as a feathered serpent. He represents the vital force of wind, creation, and is associated with Quetzalcoatl.
